abciximab.statement: All anticoagulants and thrombolytic agents can produce placental haemorrhage and subsequent prematurity and fetal loss.

abemaciclib.statement: Based on results from animal studies and its mechanism of action, abemaciclib may harm the foetus.

abiraterone.statement: Antiandrogens carry the potential for feminisation of the male fetus at or after 8 weeks post conception and exposure should be avoided during pregnancy.

acalabrutinib.statement: Based on findings in animals, acalabrutinib may cause fetal harm.

acitretin.statement: This drug is teratogenic at doses within the therapeutic range. The drug is stored in the body for several months after cessation. Because of the long half-life of this drug and storage in fat, patients are advised not to conceive until two years after cessation of treatment because of risk of birth defects. Should pregnancy occur during treatment with this drug, there is a high risk of birth defects.

actinomycin d (dactinomycin).statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

adalimumab.statement: Adalimumab crosses the placental barrier. Due to its inhibition of TNFalpha, adalimumab administered during pregnancy could affect immune response in the in utero-exposed newborn and infant.

adapalene.statement: There have been isolated reports of birth defects in babies born to women using this drug. Because of the potential risk of adverse effects on fetal development, adapalene should not be used by women who are pregnant or who plan to become pregnant during treatment.

afatinib.statement: This drug inhibits EGFR. EGFR plays an important role in embryofetal development.

albendazole.statement: In animal studies albendazole is teratogenic in several species. Until human data are available, it must be suspected of being teratogenic.

aldesleukin.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

alectinib.statement: Alectinib caused embryofetal lethality in rats and rabbits, visceral and skeletal anomalies in rats, and visceral malformation and skeletal variations in rabbits.

alfentanil.statement: Opioid analgesics may cause respiratory depression in the newborn infant. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

alimemazine.statement: When given in high doses during late pregnancy, phenothiazines have caused prolonged neurological disturbances in the infant.

aliskiren.statement: When used in pregnancy during the second and third trimesters, drugs that act directly on the renin-angiotensin system can cause injury and even death in the developing fetus. Although no adverse fetal effects have been linked to first trimester drug use of ARAs, the number of exposures reported is too small to determine conclusively that ARAs are safe in the first trimester. Pregnant women who are taking ARAs should be changed as quickly as possible to other antihypertensive medication to maintain normal blood pressure. It is generally advisable not to use ARAs for the management of hypertension in women who are likely to become pregnant.

alogliptin / metformin.statement: It is important to achieve strict normoglycaemia during pregnancy.

alpelisib.statement: Based on animal data and its mechanism of action, alpelisib can cause fetal harm when administered to a pregnant woman.

alprazolam.statement: Benzodiazepines may cause hypotonia, respiratory depression and hypothermia in the newborn infant if used in high doses during labour. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

alprenolol.statement: Beta-adrenergic blocking agents may cause pharmacological effects such as bradycardia in the fetus and newborn infant.

altretamine (hexamethylmelamine).statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

ambrisentan.statement: Teratogenicity is a class effect of these agents, which induce multiple fetal abnormalities in animal studies. These drugs are likely to cause major birth defects if used during pregnancy.

amifampridine.statement: Animal studies to assess the potential adverse effects of amifampridine  on embryofetal development have not been conducted. However, amifampridine may block potassium channels which also play a role in normal uterine smooth muscle function during gestation and parturition.

amikacin.statement: There is evidence of selective uptake of aminoglycosides by the fetal kidney resulting in damage (probably reversible) to immature nephrons. Eighth cranial nerve damage has also been reported following in utero exposure to some of the aminoglycosides. Because of their chemical similarity, all aminoglycosides must be considered potentially nephrotoxic and ototoxic to the fetus. It should also be noted that therapeutic blood concentrations in the mother do not equate with safety for the fetus.

amiloride.statement: Potassium sparing diuretics may cause an electrolyte disturbance in the fetus.

aminoglutethimide.statement: There have been reports of pseudohermaphrodism with use of this drug in pregnancy.

amiodarone.statement: Because of the long half-life of amiodarone and its major metabolite, and the potential to cause abnormal thyroid function and bradycardia in the fetus, its use is probably best avoided in the three months before and throughout the duration of pregnancy. When exposure of the fetus is unavoidable, thyroid function (including TSH) should be assessed promptly in the newborn infant.

amisulpride.statement: There have been reports of agitation, hypertonia, hypotonia, tremor, somnolence, respiratory distress and feeding disorder in these neonates. These complications have varied in severity. While in some cases symptoms have been self-limited, in other cases neonates have required intensive care unit support and prolonged hospitalization. Amisulpride should be used during pregnancy only if the potential benefit justifies the potential risk to the fetus.

amitriptyline.statement: Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged maternal use of tricyclic antidepressants.

amivantamab.statement: IgG1 is known to cross the placent. Therefore, amivantamab has the potential to be transmitted from a pregnant patient to the developing fetus. Administration of other EGFR or MET inhibitor molecules to pregnant animals has resulted in an increased incidence of impairment of embryo-fetal development, embryolethality and abortion.

amlodipine.statement: Calcium channel blockers carry the potential to produce fetal hypoxia associated with maternal hypotension.

amlodipine / atorvastatin.statement: Calcium channel blockers carry the potential to produce fetal hypoxia associated with maternal hypotension.  Cholesterol and other products of the cholesterol biosynthesis pathway are essential components for fetal development, including synthesis of steroids and cell membranes. Because of the ability of inhibitors of HMG-CoA reductase to decrease the synthesis of cholesterol and possibly other products of the cholesterol biosynthesis pathway, these drugs may cause fetal harm when administered to a pregnant woman. The physiological hyperlipidaemia of pregnancy does not require treatment.

amlodipine / hydrochlorothiazide /valsar.statement: Calcium channel blockers carry the potential to produce fetal hypoxia associated with maternal hypotension.  When used in pregnancy during the second and third trimesters, drugs that act directly on the renin-angiotensin system can cause injury and even death in the developing fetus. Although no adverse fetal effects have been linked to first trimester drug use of ARAs, the number of exposures reported is too small to determine conclusively that ARAs are safe in the first trimester. Pregnant women who are taking ARAs should be changed as quickly as possible to other antihypertensive medication to maintain normal blood pressure. It is generally advisable not to use ARAs for the management of hypertension in women who are likely to become pregnant. Thiazides, related diuretics and loop diuretics may cause electrolyte disturbances in the fetus. Neonatal thrombocytopenia has been reported with thiazides and related diuretics. Loop diuretics, like frusemide and bumetanide, are probably also associated with this risk. During the latter part of pregnancy products of this type should only be given on sound indications, and then in the lowest effective dose.

amlodipine / olmesartan medoxomil.statement: Calcium channel blockers carry the potential to produce fetal hypoxia associated with maternal hypotension.  When used in pregnancy during the second and third trimesters, drugs that act directly on the renin-angiotensin system can cause injury and even death in the developing fetus. Although no adverse fetal effects have been linked to first trimester drug use of ARAs, the number of exposures reported is too small to determine conclusively that ARAs are safe in the first trimester. Pregnant women who are taking ARAs should be changed as quickly as possible to other antihypertensive medication to maintain normal blood pressure. It is generally advisable not to use ARAs for the management of hypertension in women who are likely to become pregnant.

When used in pregnancy during the second and third trimesters, drugs that act directly on the renin-angiotensin system can cause injury and even death in the developing fetus. When used in pregnancy during the second and third trimesters, drugs that act directly on the renin-angiotensin system can cause injury and even death in the developing fetus. Although no adverse fetal effects have been linked to first trimester drug use of ARAs, the number of exposures reported is too small to determine conclusively that ARAs are safe in the first trimester. Pregnant women who are taking ARAs should be changed as quickly as possible to other antihypertensive medication to maintain normal blood pressure. It is generally advisable not to use ARAs for the management of hypertension in women who are likely to become pregnant.

amlodipine / valsartan.statement: Calcium channel blockers carry the potential to produce fetal hypoxia associated with maternal hypotension.  When used in pregnancy during the second and third trimesters, drugs that act directly on the renin-angiotensin system can cause injury and even death in the developing fetus. Although no adverse fetal effects have been linked to first trimester drug use of ARAs, the number of exposures reported is too small to determine conclusively that ARAs are safe in the first trimester. Pregnant women who are taking ARAs should be changed as quickly as possible to other antihypertensive medication to maintain normal blood pressure. It is generally advisable not to use ARAs for the management of hypertension in women who are likely to become pregnant.

amobarbital.statement: Barbiturates can give rise to hypotension, respiratory depression and hypothermia in the newborn infant. Continuous treatment during pregnancy and administration during labour should be avoided.

amsacrine.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

amylobarbitone (amobarbital).statement: Barbiturates can give rise to hypotension, respiratory depression and hypothermia in the newborn infant. Continuous treatment during pregnancy and administration during labour should be avoided.

anastrozole.statement: This drug disrupts oestrogen dependant metabolism and may result in abortion.

anifrolumab.statement: A higher risk of certain viral infections could theoretically occur in (human) infants exposed to anifrolumab during the later stage of the gestational period.

apalutamide.statement: Based on its mechanism of action, it may cause fetal harm when administered during pregnancy.

apixaban.statement: All anticoagulants and thrombolytic agents can produce placental haemorrhage and subsequent prematurity and fetal loss.

aripiprazole.statement: There have been reports of agitation, hypertonia, hypotonia, tremor, somnolence, respiratory distress and feeding disorder in these neonates. These complications have varied in severity. While in some cases symptoms have been self-limited, in other cases neonates have required intensive care unit support and prolonged hospitalization. Aripiprazole should be used during pregnancy only if the potential benefit justifies the potential risk to the fetus.

armodafinil.statement: This drug is embryotoxic and fetotoxic when administered to pregnant rats and rabbits.

arsenic trioxide.statement: In hamsters, rats and mice, parenteral administration of arsenite during the period of organogenesis produced fetal malformations, including neural tube, eye, facial, genitourinary and skeletal defects. Arsenite treatment of mice during gestation has also produced a widespread tumourigenic response in offspring.

artemether with lumefantine.statement: Artemisinins are known to be embryotoxic in animals, causing cardiovascular and skeletal malformations. Based on evidence from animal studies, artemether is suspected to cause serious birth defects when administered during the first trimester of pregnancy.

asciminib hydrochloride.statement: Asciminib may cause fetal harm when administered to a pregnant woman. In treated pregnant rats and rabbits, administration of asciminib during organogenesis induced embryotoxicity, fetotoxicity and teratogenicity.

asenapine.statement: There have been reports of agitation, hypertonia, hypotonia, tremor, somnolence, respiratory distress and feeding disorder in these neonates. These complications have varied in severity. While in some cases symptoms have been self-limited, in other cases neonates have required intensive care unit support and prolonged hospitalization. Asenapine should be used during pregnancy only if the potential benefit justifies the potential risk to the fetus.

asfotase alfa (rch).statement: There is a potential effect on embryofetal and neonate bone development with the production of antibodies against asfotase alfa.

asparaginase.statement: Cytotoxicity from asparaginase activity poses a risk for spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

aspirin.statement: Aspirin inhibits prostaglandin synthesis. When given late in pregnancy, it may cause premature closure of the fetal ductus arteriosus, delay labour and birth. Aspirin increases the bleeding time both in the newborn infant and in the mother because of its antiplatelet effects. Products containing aspirin should be avoided in the last trimester. Low-dose aspirin (100mg/day) does not affect bleeding time.

aspirin / dipyridamole.statement: Aspirin inhibits prostaglandin synthesis. When given late in pregnancy, it may cause premature closure of the fetal ductus arteriosus, delay labour and birth. Aspirin increases the bleeding time both in the newborn infant and in the mother because of its antiplatelet effects. Products containing aspirin should be avoided in the last trimester. Low-dose aspirin (100mg/day) does not affect bleeding time.

atenolol.statement: Beta-adrenergic blocking agents may cause pharmacological effects such as bradycardia in the fetus and newborn infant.

atezolizumab.statement: PD-1/PD-L1 is involved in fetomaternal tolerance. Administration during pregnancy may cause fetal harm, including abortion or stillbirth.

atorvastatin.statement: Cholesterol and other products of the cholesterol biosynthesis pathway are essential components for fetal development, including synthesis of steroids and cell membranes. Because of the ability of inhibitors of HMG-CoA reductase to decrease the synthesis of cholesterol and possibly other products of the cholesterol biosynthesis pathway, these drugs may cause fetal harm when administered to a pregnant woman. The physiological hyperlipidaemia of pregnancy does not require treatment.

atorvastatin / ezetimibe.statement: Cholesterol and other products of the cholesterol biosynthesis pathway are essential components for fetal development, including synthesis of steroids and cell membranes. Because of the ability of inhibitors of HMG-CoA reductase to decrease the synthesis of cholesterol and possibly other products of the cholesterol biosynthesis pathway, these drugs may cause fetal harm when administered to a pregnant woman. The physiological hyperlipidaemia of pregnancy does not require treatment.

atracurium.statement: There have been no demonstrated adverse effects in the fetus or the newborn infant.

avacopan.statement: Oral administration of avacopan to pregnant hamsters during the period of organogenesis caused an increased incidence of skeletal variations in the absence of maternal toxicity, while in rabbits an increase in the number of abortions was observed at subclinical maternal exposures.

avelumab.statement: PD-1/PD-L1 is involved in fetomaternal tolerance. Administration during pregnancy may cause fetal harm, including abortion or stillbirth.

axicabtagene ciloleucel.statement: Based on the mechanism of action, if the transduced cells cross the placenta, they may cause fetal toxicity, including B-cell lymphocytopaenia.

axitinib.statement: Angiogenesis plays a key role in embryo-fetal development.VEGF receptor inhibitors inhibit angiogenesis and may cause fetal harm when administered to pregnant women.

azacitidine.statement: Azacitidine must not be used during pregnancy. There are no adequate data on the use of azacitidine in pregnant women. Studies in animals have shown reproductive toxicity including teratogenic effects at relatively low doses. Azacitidine also had adverse effects on embryofetal development following treatment of male rats.

azathioprine.statement: This drug has been associated with a slightly increased risk of fetal malformations, neonatal immunosuppression and bone marrow suppression in the infant.

balsalazide.statement: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs inhibit prostaglandin synthesis and, when given during the latter part of pregnancy, may cause closure of the fetal ductus arteriosus, fetal renal impairment, inhibition of platelet aggregation, and delay labour and birth. Continuous treatment with non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs during the last trimester of pregnancy should only be given on sound indications. During the last few days before expected birth, agents with an inhibitory effect on prostaglandin synthesis should be avoided.

baricitinib.statement: Based on the mechanism of action, baricitinib is suspected to affect early embryonic development when administered during pregnancy. Baricitinib has been shown to cause embryofetal lethality and fetal malformations in pregnant rats or rabbits.

basiliximab.statement: This drug has potentially hazardous pharmacological effects based on its immunosuppressive action.

beclometasone.statement: The benefits of asthma control outweigh any potential for an adverse pregnancy outcome.

beclomethasone.statement: The benefits of asthma control outweigh any potential for an adverse pregnancy outcome.

belantamab mafodotin.statement: Based on the mechanism of action of the cytotoxic component monomethyl auristatin F, belantamab mafodotin can cause embryo-fetal harm when administered to a pregnant woman. The cytotoxic component kills rapidly dividing cells, which would affect a developing embryo. There is also a potential risk of heritable changes via aneuploidy in female germ cells.

belimumab.statement: IgG antibodies are known to cross the placental barrier. This drug has the potential to affect embryofetal survival due to its pharmacological action.

belzutifan.statement: Based on findings in animal studies, belzutifan may cause fetal harm, including fetal loss - administration of belzutifan to pregnant rats during organogenesis caused embryo-fetal lethality, reduced fetal body weight and fetal skeletal abnormalities.

bendamustine hydrochloride.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

bendrofluazide.statement: Thiazides, related diuretics and loop diuretics may cause electrolyte disturbances in the fetus. Neonatal thrombocytopenia has been reported with thiazides and related diuretics. Loop diuretics, like frusemide and bumetanide, are probably also associated with this risk. During the latter part of pregnancy products of this type should only be given on sound indications, and then in the lowest effective dose.

benzoyl peroxide / adapalene.statement: There have been isolated reports of birth defects in babies born to women using this drug. Because of the potential risk of adverse effects on fetal development, adapalene should not be used by women who are pregnant or who plan to become pregnant during treatment.

betaxolol.statement: Beta-adrenergic blocking agents may cause pharmacological effects such as bradycardia in the fetus and newborn infant.

betaxolol (ophthalmic).statement: Beta-adrenergic blocking agents may cause pharmacological effects such as bradycardia in the fetus and newborn infant.

bethanechol.statement: Bethanechol has a potent excitatory effect on smooth muscle and should be avoided during pregnancy.

bevacizumab.statement: IgG antibodies are known to cross the placental barrier, and this drug may inhibit angiogenesis in the fetus. Angiogenesis has been shown to be critically important to fetal development.

bevantolol.statement: Beta-adrenergic blocking agents may cause pharmacological effects such as bradycardia in the fetus and newborn infant.

bicalutamide.statement: Oral administration of this drug to rats in late gestation and during lactation caused feminisation of the male offspring.

bimatoprost / timolol.statement: Beta-adrenergic blocking agents may cause pharmacological effects such as bradycardia in the fetus and newborn infant.

bimekizumab.statement: Based on the mechanism of action of bimekizumab, the theoretical risk that use during pregnancy may affect neonatal immunity cannot be excluded.

binimetinib.statement: Based on results from animal studies, binimetinib may harm the foetus. Binimetinib should not be administered during pregnancy unless the benefits for the mother clearly outweigh the risks for the fetus.

bisoprolol.statement: Beta-adrenergic blocking agents may cause pharmacological effects such as bradycardia in the fetus and newborn infant.

bivalirudin.statement: All anticoagulants and thrombolytic agents can produce placental haemorrhage and subsequent prematurity and fetal loss.

bleomycin.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

boceprevir with ribavirin and peginterfe.statement: Although there are no pertinent human data, ribavirin has been found to be teratogenic and/or embryolethal in nearly all species in which it has been tested. Malformations of skull, palate, eye, jaw, skeleton and gastrointestinal tract were noted in animal studies. Survival of fetuses and offspring was reduced.

bortezomib.statement: If sufficient placental transfer occurred, bortezomib has the potential to disrupt protein cycling in the developing fetus and, therefore, may have an adverse effect on fetal development.

bosentan.statement: Teratogenicity is a class effect of these agents, which induce multiple fetal abnormalities in animal studies. These drugs are likely to cause major birth defects if used during pregnancy.

brentuximab vedotin.statement: IgG antibodies are known to cross the placental barrier and this drug binds to the epidermal growth factor receptor that is involved in fetal development.

bretylium tosilate.statement: This drug carries the potential for fetal hypoxia associated with maternal hypotension.

brexpiprazole.statement: Neonates exposed to antipsychotic drugs during the third trimester of pregnancy are at risk of extrapyramidal and/or withdrawal symptoms following delivery. There have been reports of agitation, hypertonia, hypotonia, tremor, somnolence, respiratory distress and feeding disorder in these neonates. These complications have varied in severity; while in some cases symptoms have been self-limited, in other cases neonates have required intensive care unit support and prolonged hospitalisation.

brexucabtagene autoleucel.statement: Based on the mechanism of action, if the transduced cells cross the placenta, they may cause fetal toxicity, including B-cell lymphocytopaenia.

brigatinib.statement: Brigatinib may cause fetal harm when administered to a pregnant woman. Studies in animals have shown reproductive toxicity. In treated pregnant rats at doses approximately equivalent to the human dose, skeletal or visceral anomalies and malformations.

brimonidine / timolol.statement: Beta-adrenergic blocking agents may cause pharmacological effects such as bradycardia in the fetus and newborn infant.

brinzolamide / timolol.statement: Beta-adrenergic blocking agents may cause pharmacological effects such as bradycardia in the fetus and newborn infant.

brolucizumab.statement: Based on the mechanism of action, brolucizumab is regarded as potentially teratogenic and embryo/fetotoxic.

bromazepam.statement: Benzodiazepines may cause hypotonia, respiratory depression and hypothermia in the newborn infant if used in high doses during labour. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

budesonide.statement: The benefits of asthma control outweigh any potential for an adverse pregnancy outcome.

bumetanide.statement: Thiazides, related diuretics and loop diuretics may cause electrolyte disturbances in the fetus. Neonatal thrombocytopenia has been reported with thiazides and related diuretics. Loop diuretics, like frusemide and bumetanide, are probably also associated with this risk. During the latter part of pregnancy products of this type should only be given on sound indications, and then in the lowest effective dose.

bupivacaine / fentanyl.statement: Opioid analgesics may cause respiratory depression in the newborn infant. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

buprenorphine.statement: Opioid analgesics may cause respiratory depression in the newborn infant. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

busulfan.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

cabazitaxel.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

cabozantinib.statement: Animal studies in rats or rabbits with cabozantinib have shown adverse effects of embryofetal lethality and and teratogenicity.

canagliflozin.statement: Studies in rats have shown toxicity to the developing kidney in the time period corresponding to the second and third trimesters of human pregnancy.

candesartan cilexetil.statement: When used in pregnancy during the second and third trimesters, drugs that act directly on the renin-angiotensin system can cause injury and even death in the developing fetus. Although no adverse fetal effects have been linked to first trimester drug use of ARAs, the number of exposures reported is too small to determine conclusively that ARAs are safe in the first trimester. Pregnant women who are taking ARAs should be changed as quickly as possible to other antihypertensive medication to maintain normal blood pressure. It is generally advisable not to use ARAs for the management of hypertension in women who are likely to become pregnant.

capecitabine.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

capivasertib.statement: PI3K/AKT/mTOR signalling plays important roles in embryofetal development. Akt knockout results in a range of fetal defects in mice. Capivasertib caused an increase in early embryonic deaths, and fetal visceral abormalities in rats.

captopril.statement: When taken during the second and third trimesters, ACE inhibitors cause a range of abnormalities including renal dysfunction and oligohydramnios. These can be associated with fetal death in utero. Although no adverse fetal effects have been linked to first trimester drug use of ACE inhibitors, the number of exposures reported is too small to determine conclusively that ACE inhibitors are safe in the first trimester.  Pregnant women who are taking ACE inhibitors should be changed as quickly as possible to other antihypertensive medication to maintain normal blood pressure.  It is generally advisable not to use ACE inhibitors for the management of hypertension in women who are likely to become pregnant.

carbimazole.statement: Antithyroid agents may cause congenital goitre by inhibiting thyroxine synthesis in the fetus.

carboplatin.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

carboprost.statement: This drug has been found to cross the placenta and distribute to the fetus in pregnant women. Administration of prostaglandings including this drug during pregnancy stimulates the uterus and may cause inability to sustain pregnancy and irreversible fetal damage or death.

carfilzomib.statement: Based on its mechanism of action and findings in animals, carfilzomib can cause foetal harm when administered to a pregnant woman

cariprazine.statement: Malformations: Dosing of cariprazine to rats caused malformations, lower pup survival, and developmental delays. Class effect: Neonates exposed to antipsychotics (including cariprazine) during the third trimester of pregnancy are at risk of adverse reactions including extrapyramidal and/or withdrawal symptoms that may vary in severity and duration following delivery. There have been reports of agitation, hypertonia, hypotonia, tremor, somnolence, respiratory distress or feeding disorder. These complications have varied in severity; while in some cases symptoms have been self-limited, in other cases, neonates have required intensive care unit support and prolonged hospitalisation. Consequently, newborns should be monitored carefully.

carmustine.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

carvedilol.statement: Beta-adrenergic blocking agents may cause pharmacological effects such as bradycardia in the fetus and newborn infant.

celecoxib.statement:
Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory (NSAIDs) agents inhibit prostaglandin synthesis which may adversely affect pregnancy. Refer to the relevant texts in the Product Information of individual NSAID products for information regarding the suitability for use in pregnancy. 

Epidemiological studies suggest an increased risk of spontaneous abortion after use of prostaglandin synthesis inhibitors in early pregnancy.

When given during the latter part of pregnancy, may cause closure of the fetal ductus arteriosus, oligohydramnios, fetal renal impairment, inhibition of platelet aggregation, and delay labour and birth.

Continuous treatment with NSAIDs during the last trimester of pregnancy should only be given on sound indications. During the last few days before expected birth, agents with an inhibitory effect on prostaglandin synthesis should be avoided.

cemiplimab.statement: PD-1/PD-L1 is involved in fetomaternal tolerance. Administration during pregnancy may cause fetal harm, including abortion or stillbirth.

ceritinib.statement: As an ALK inhibitor, ceritinib also inhibits other members of the insulin receptor superfamily, such as InsR, IGF-1R and ROS1. Since InsR and IGF-1R play vital roles during early development including embryofetal development and postnatal growth, ceritinib is likely to adversely affect embryofetal development.

cerivastatin.statement: Cholesterol and other products of the cholesterol biosynthesis pathway are essential components for fetal development, including synthesis of steroids and cell membranes. Because of the ability of inhibitors of HMG-CoA reductase to decrease the synthesis of cholesterol and possibly other products of the cholesterol biosynthesis pathway, these drugs may cause fetal harm when administered to a pregnant woman. The physiological hyperlipidaemia of pregnancy does not require treatment.

certolizumab.statement: Due to its inhibition of TNF?, certolizumab administration during pregnancy could affect normal immune responses in the new-born.

cetrorelix.statement: There is a theoretical risk of abortion if gonadotrophin releasing hormone antagonists are used during pregnancy.

cetuximab.statement: IgG antibodies are known to cross the placental barrier and this drug binds to the epidermal growth factor receptor that is involved in fetal development.

chlorambucil.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

chlordiazepoxide.statement: Benzodiazepines may cause hypotonia, respiratory depression and hypothermia in the newborn infant if used in high doses during labour. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

chlormethine.statement: Pregnancy Category B3 applies only to topical use.

chloroquine (prophylaxis).statement: The use of this drug in the treatment of malaria is accepted because the small risk to the fetus is outweighed by the benefits to the mother and fetus.  Prophylaxis in high risk situations is also justified.

chloroquine (treatment).statement: When used in high doses and for prolonged periods, chloroquine and related substances may cause neurological disturbances and interference with hearing, balance and vision in the fetus. The use of this drug in the treatment of malaria is accepted because the small risk to the fetus is outweighed by the benefits to the mother and fetus.  Prophylaxis in high risk situations is also justified.

chlorothiazide.statement: Thiazides, related diuretics and loop diuretics may cause electrolyte disturbances in the fetus. Neonatal thrombocytopenia has been reported with thiazides and related diuretics. Loop diuretics, like frusemide and bumetanide, are probably also associated with this risk. During the latter part of pregnancy products of this type should only be given on sound indications, and then in the lowest effective dose.

chlorpromazine.statement: When given in high doses during late pregnancy, phenothiazines have caused prolonged neurological disturbances in the newborn infant.

chlorpropamide.statement: Sulphonylureas may enter the fetal circulation and may cause neonatal hypoglycaemia.

chlortalidone.statement: Thiazides, related diuretics and loop diuretics may cause electrolyte disturbances in the fetus. Neonatal thrombocytopenia has been reported with thiazides and related diuretics. Loop diuretics, like frusemide and bumetanide, are probably also associated with this risk. During the latter part of pregnancy products of this type should only be given on sound indications, and then in the lowest effective dose.

ciclosporin.statement: This drug may cause immunosuppression in the infant.

cidofovir.statement: This drug could be expected to cause fetal loss and birth defects.

cilazapril.statement: When taken during the second and third trimesters, ACE inhibitors cause a range of abnormalities including renal dysfunction and oligohydramnios. These can be associated with fetal death in utero. Although no adverse fetal effects have been linked to first trimester drug use of ACE inhibitors, the number of exposures reported is too small to determine conclusively that ACE inhibitors are safe in the first trimester.  Pregnant women who are taking ACE inhibitors should be changed as quickly as possible to other antihypertensive medication to maintain normal blood pressure.  It is generally advisable not to use ACE inhibitors for the management of hypertension in women who are likely to become pregnant.

ciltacabtagene autoleucel.statement: Based on the mechanism of action, ciltacabtagene autoleucel may cause fetal harm, including  B-cell lymphocytopenia, when administered to a pregnant woman. Fetal B-cell depletion poses a risk of opportunistic infections in the neonate. Ciltacabtagene autoleucel-induced cytokine release may also pose a risk for embryofetal loss.

cisatracurium besilate.statement: There have been no demonstrated adverse effects in the fetus or the newborn infant.

cisplatin.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

citalopram.statement: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) have had limited use in pregnancy without a reported increase in birth defects.  The use of SSRIs in the third trimester may result in a withdrawal state in the newborn infant.

cladribine.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

clascoterone.statement: Administration of clascoterone during pregnancy in rats and rabbits produced adverse effects on development including embryolethality and fetal malformations.

clevidipine.statement: This drug caused increases in maternal and fetal mortality and length of gestation in animals.

clindamycin / tretinoin.statement: Use of tretinoin cream formulation during the first trimester does not appear to cause birth defects. Other formulations should not be used during pregnancy. There have been isolated reports of birth defects in babies born to women using topical tretinoin in pregnancy, some similar to those reported with oral retinoids. While a retrospective cohort study on women exposed to tretinoin in the first trimester did not reveal an association with this treatment, the numbers in this study are too small to establish the safety of use in pregnancy.

clobazam.statement: Benzodiazepines may cause hypotonia, respiratory depression and hypothermia in the newborn infant if used in high doses during labour. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

clofarabine.statement: Clofarabine may cause fetal harm when administered to a pregnant woman. The drug was teratogenic in rats and rabbits.

clofazimine.statement: Clofazimine may cause discolouration of the skin of the baby. This is reversible but recovery may be delayed because clofazimine has an average serum half life of 70 days.

clofibrate.statement: The physiological hyperlipidaemia of pregnancy does not require treatment.

clomipramine.statement: Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged maternal use of tricyclic antidepressants.

clonazepam.statement: Benzodiazepines may cause hypotonia, respiratory depression and hypothermia in the newborn infant if used in high doses during labour. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

clopamide.statement: Thiazides, related diuretics and loop diuretics may cause electrolyte disturbances in the fetus. Neonatal thrombocytopenia has been reported with thiazides and related diuretics. Loop diuretics, like frusemide and bumetanide, are probably also associated with this risk. During the latter part of pregnancy products of this type should only be given on sound indications, and then in the lowest effective dose.

clopidogrel.statement: All anticoagulants and thrombolytic agents can produce placental haemorrhage and subsequent prematurity and fetal loss.

clopidogrel / aspirin.statement: Aspirin inhibits prostaglandin synthesis. When given late in pregnancy, it may cause premature closure of the fetal ductus arteriosus, delay labour and birth. Aspirin increases the bleeding time both in the newborn infant and in the mother because of its antiplatelet effects. Products containing aspirin should be avoided in the last trimester. Low-dose aspirin (100mg/day) does not affect bleeding time.

clorazepate.statement: Benzodiazepines may cause hypotonia, respiratory depression and hypothermia in the newborn infant if used in high doses during labour. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

clozapine.statement: The adverse pharmacological and toxicological effects of clozapine in adults may also occur in the fetus.

cobimetinib fumarate.statement: Drugs which have caused, are suspected to have caused or may be expected to cause, an increased incidence of human fetal malformations or irreversible damage. These drugs may also have adverse pharmacological effects. Accompanying texts should be consulted for further details.

colaspase (asparaginase).statement: Cytotoxicity from asparaginase activity poses a risk for spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

colchicine.statement: Cell division can be arrested by colchicine. This drug should be avoided in pregnancy.

colestipol.statement: The physiological hyperlipidaemia of pregnancy does not require treatment.

colestyramine.statement: The physiological hyperlipidaemia of pregnancy does not require treatment.

crisantaspase.statement: Cytotoxicity from asparaginase activity poses a risk for spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

crizotinib.statement: Crizotinib has pharmacological activity against c-Met and RON kinase, both of which play a role in embryofetal development

cyclopenthiazide.statement: Thiazides, related diuretics and loop diuretics may cause electrolyte disturbances in the fetus. Neonatal thrombocytopenia has been reported with thiazides and related diuretics. Loop diuretics, like frusemide and bumetanide, are probably also associated with this risk. During the latter part of pregnancy products of this type should only be given on sound indications, and then in the lowest effective dose.

cyclophosphamide.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

cyproterone acetate (10 mg daily or high.statement: Antiandrogens carry the potential for feminisation of the male fetus at or after 8 weeks post conception and should be avoided during pregnancy. Administration of high doses of cyproterone acetate during the hormone sensitive differentiation phase of the genital organs (after approximately day 45 of pregnancy) could lead to signs of feminisation in male fetuses.

cyproterone acetate (2 mg daily po).statement: Antiandrogens carry the potential for feminisation of the male fetus at or after 8 weeks post conception and should be avoided during pregnancy.

cytarabine.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

dabigatran etexilate.statement: All anticoagulants and thrombolytic agents can produce placental haemorrhage and subsequent prematurity and fetal loss.

dabrafenib mesilate.statement: This drug inhibits multiple kinases (including BRAF) that may be important during embryofetal development.

dacarbazine.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

dactinomycin.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

dalteparin.statement: All anticoagulants and thrombolytic agents can produce placental haemorrhage and subsequent prematurity and fetal loss.

danaparoid.statement: All anticoagulants and thrombolytic agents can produce placental haemorrhage and subsequent prematurity and fetal loss.

danazol.statement: If taken by the mother at or after 8 weeks post conception, danazol may cause virilisation of the female fetus. Prior to 8 weeks post conception it has no virilising effects. Danazol may not inhibit ovulation in all women.

dapagliflozin.statement: Studies in rats have shown toxicity to the developing kidney in the time period corresponding to the second and third trimesters of human pregnancy.

dapagliflozin / saxagliptin.statement: Studies with dapagliflozin in rats have shown toxicity to the developing kidney in the time period corresponding to the second and third trimesters of human pregnancy.

dapagliflozin /metformin.statement: Studies with dapagliflozin in rats have shown toxicity to the developing kidney in the time period corresponding to the second and third trimesters of human pregnancy.

dapoxetine.statement: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) have had limited use in pregnancy without a reported increase in birth defects.  The use of SSRIs in the third trimester may result in a withdrawal state in the newborn infant.

daratumumab.statement: IgG antibodies are known to cross the placental barrier. This drug has the potential to affect embryofetal survival due to its pharmacological action.

darolutamide.statement: Based on its mechanism of action, it may cause fetal harm when administered during pregnancy.

dasatinib.statement: This drug is embryotoxic in both pregnant rats and rabbits, and teratogenic when administered to pregnant animals.

daunorubicin.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

decitabine / cedazuridine.statement: Decitabine / cedazuridine is contraindicated in pregnancy. Decitabine has been shown to cause malformations and embryofetal lethality in multiple laboratory animal species at dose levels well below the clinical dose.

deferiprone.statement: Reproductive studies in non iron-loaded rats and rabbits have indicated that deferiprone is embryotoxic and teratogenic.

defibrotide.statement: This drug has revealed a high rate of haemorrhagic abortion in animals.

degarelix.statement: At very low doses (<10% clinical dose), an increase in the number of abortions, early embryo/fetal deaths and premature deliveries along with prolonged parturition were observed in animals

demeclocycline.statement: Tetracyclines are safe for use during the first 18 weeks of pregnancy (16 weeks post conception) after which they cause discolouration of the baby’s teeth.

denosumab.statement: Treatment with denosumab may impair bone growth in children with open growth plates and may inhibit eruption of dentition.

desflurane.statement: All general anaesthetics carry the potential to produce central nervous system and respiratory depression in the newborn infant. In routine practice this does not appear to be a problem. However, in the compromised fetus, careful consideration should be given to this potential depression and to the selection of particular anaesthetic drugs, doses and techniques.

desipramine.statement: Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged maternal use of tricyclic antidepressants.

desirudin.statement: All anticoagulants and thrombolytic agents can produce placental haemorrhage and subsequent prematurity and fetal loss.

dexfenfluramine.statement: Weight reduction using appetite suppressant drugs is not recommended in pregnancy.

dextromoramide.statement: Opioid analgesics may cause respiratory depression in the newborn infant. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

dextropropoxyphene.statement: Opioid analgesics may cause respiratory depression in the newborn infant. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

diazepam.statement: Benzodiazepines may cause hypotonia, respiratory depression and hypothermia in the newborn infant if used in high doses during labour. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

diazoxide.statement: This drug may cause fetal bradycardia. Hyperglycaemia has been observed in the newborn. Diazoxide is a potent relaxant of uterine smooth muscle and may inhibit uterine contraction if given during labour. Diazoxide should be used with extreme caution during pregnancy.

diclofenac.statement:
Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory (NSAIDs) agents inhibit prostaglandin synthesis which may adversely affect pregnancy. Refer to the relevant texts in the Product Information of individual NSAID products for information regarding the suitability for use in pregnancy. 

Epidemiological studies suggest an increased risk of spontaneous abortion after use of prostaglandin synthesis inhibitors in early pregnancy.

When given during the latter part of pregnancy, may cause closure of the fetal ductus arteriosus, oligohydramnios, fetal renal impairment, inhibition of platelet aggregation, and delay labour and birth.

Continuous treatment with NSAIDs during the last trimester of pregnancy should only be given on sound indications. During the last few days before expected birth, agents with an inhibitory effect on prostaglandin synthesis should be avoided.

diethylpropion.statement: Weight reduction using appetite suppressant drugs is not recommended in pregnancy.

diflunisal.statement:
Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory (NSAIDs) agents inhibit prostaglandin synthesis which may adversely affect pregnancy. Refer to the relevant texts in the Product Information of individual NSAID products for information regarding the suitability for use in pregnancy. 

Epidemiological studies suggest an increased risk of spontaneous abortion after use of prostaglandin synthesis inhibitors in early pregnancy.

When given during the latter part of pregnancy, may cause closure of the fetal ductus arteriosus, oligohydramnios, fetal renal impairment, inhibition of platelet aggregation, and delay labour and birth.

Continuous treatment with NSAIDs during the last trimester of pregnancy should only be given on sound indications. During the last few days before expected birth, agents with an inhibitory effect on prostaglandin synthesis should be avoided.

dihydroergotamine.statement: Standard oral dose regimens for migraine headaches in the first half of pregnancy do not appear to pose hazards to the fetus. Ergotamine induces uterine contraction and may therefore cause premature parturition or hypertonic labour. Larger doses or more frequent use may jeopardise the fetus because of the potential for impeding fetal blood supply.

diltiazem.statement: Calcium channel blockers carry the potential to produce fetal hypoxia associated with maternal hypotension.

dinoprostone (prostaglandin e2).statement: There have been instances of idiosyncratic sensitivity of the uterus resulting in fetal anoxia.

dinutuximab beta.statement: Based on the mechanism of action, dinutuximab beta has the potential to affect embryofetal development and cause fetal harm.

diphenoxylate.statement: This drug is chemically related to the narcotic analgesic pethidine. Narcotic analgesics may cause respiratory depression in the newborn infant. This drug should not be given at or near term.

diphtheria-tetanus-pertussis-hepatitis b.category: B1 or B2 (depending on brand of vaccine)

docetaxel.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

dofetilide.statement: This drug has been shown to cause fetal deths and abnormalities when administered to animals during gestation.

dorzolamide / timolol.statement: Beta-adrenergic blocking agents may cause pharmacological effects such as bradycardia in the fetus and newborn infant.

dostarlimab.statement: PD-1/PD-L1 is involved in fetomaternal tolerance. Administration during pregnancy may cause fetal harm, including abortion or stillbirth.

dosulepin.statement: Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged maternal use of tricyclic antidepressants.

dothiepin (dosulepin).statement: Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged maternal use of tricyclic antidepressants.

doxepin.statement: Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged maternal use of tricyclic antidepressants.

doxorubicin.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

doxycycline.statement: Tetracyclines are safe for use during the first 18 weeks of pregnancy (16 weeks post conception) after which they cause discolouration of the baby’s teeth.

doxycycline (antimalarial).statement: Tetracyclines are safe for use during the first 18 weeks of pregnancy (16 weeks post conception) after which they cause discolouration of the baby’s teeth. The use of this drug in the treatment of malaria is accepted because the small risk to the fetus is outweighed by the benefits to the mother and fetus.  Prophylaxis in high risk situations is also justified.

droperidol.statement: When given in high doses during late pregnancy, butyrophenones may cause prolonged neurological disturbances in the newborn infant.

duloxetine.statement: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) have had limited use in pregnancy without a reported increase in birth defects.  The use of SSRIs in the third trimester may result in a withdrawal state in the newborn infant.

durvalumab.statement: PD-1/PD-L1 is involved in fetomaternal tolerance. Administration during pregnancy may cause fetal harm, including abortion or stillbirth.

dutasteride.statement: Dutasteride may cause abnormalities of the external genitalia of a male fetus.

efavirenz.statement: This drug may cause fetal harm when administered during the first trimester to a pregnant woman. It crossed the placenta and was teratogenic (anencephaly, unilateral anophthalmia, microophthalmia and/or cleft palate) in cynomolgus monkeys.

elotuzumab.statement: Based on its pharmacology, maternal treatment with elotuzumab may have effects on fetal immune cells and maternal tolerance.

elranatamab.statement: Based on the mechanism of action, elranatamab may cause fetal harm, including  B-cell lymphocytopenia, when administered to a pregnant woman. Fetal B-cell depletion poses a risk of opportunistic infections in the neonate. Elranatamab-induced cytokine release may also pose a risk for embryofetal loss.

empagliflozin.statement: Studies in rats have shown toxicity to the developing kidney in the time period corresponding to the second and third trimesters of human pregnancy.

empagliflozin / linagliptin.statement: Studies with empagliflozin in rats have shown toxicity to the developing kidney in the time period corresponding to the second and third trimesters of human pregnancy.

empagliflozin / metformin.statement: Studies with empagliflozin in rats have shown toxicity to the developing kidney in the time period corresponding to the second and third trimesters of human pregnancy.

enalapril.statement: When taken during the second and third trimesters, ACE inhibitors cause a range of abnormalities including renal dysfunction and oligohydramnios. These can be associated with fetal death in utero. Although no adverse fetal effects have been linked to first trimester drug use of ACE inhibitors, the number of exposures reported is too small to determine conclusively that ACE inhibitors are safe in the first trimester.  Pregnant women who are taking ACE inhibitors should be changed as quickly as possible to other antihypertensive medication to maintain normal blood pressure.  It is generally advisable not to use ACE inhibitors for the management of hypertension in women who are likely to become pregnant.

enasidenib.statement: Enasidenib can cause embryofetal harm when administered to a pregnant woman based on adverse findings in animal studies.

encorafenib.statement: Based on results from animal studies, encorafenib may harm the fetus. Encorafenib should not be administered during pregnancy unless the benefits for the mother outweigh the risks for the fetus. If encorafenib is used during pregnancy or if the patient becomes pregnant while taking encorafenib, the patient should be informed of the potential hazard to the fetus.

enflurane.statement: All general anaesthetics carry the potential to produce central nervous system and respiratory depression in the newborn infant. In routine practice this does not appear to be a problem. However, in the compromised fetus, careful consideration should be given to this potential depression and to the selection of particular anaesthetic drugs, doses and techniques.

enfortumab vedotin.statement: Based on its mechanism of action and findings in animals, enfortumab vedotin can cause fetal harm.

enoxaparin.statement: All anticoagulants and thrombolytic agents can produce placental haemorrhage and subsequent prematurity and fetal loss.

entrectinib.statement: Based on its mechanism of action and findings in animal studies, entrectinib may cause fetal malformations.

enzalutamide.statement: Teratogenicity (cleft palate, cervical rib and decreased anogenital distance) and embryofetal lethality were seen in a mouse embryofetal development study.

epcoritamab.statement: Based on the mechanism of action, epcoritamab may cause fetal harm, including  B-cell lymphocytopenia, when administered to a pregnant woman. Fetal B-cell depletion poses a risk of opportunistic infections in the neonate. Epcoritamab-induced cytokine release may also pose a risk for embryofetal loss.

epirubicin.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

eprosartan.statement: When used in pregnancy during the second and third trimesters, drugs that act directly on the renin-angiotensin system can cause injury and even death in the developing fetus. Although no adverse fetal effects have been linked to first trimester drug use of ARAs, the number of exposures reported is too small to determine conclusively that ARAs are safe in the first trimester. Pregnant women who are taking ARAs should be changed as quickly as possible to other antihypertensive medication to maintain normal blood pressure. It is generally advisable not to use ARAs for the management of hypertension in women who are likely to become pregnant.

eptifibatide.statement: All anticoagulants and thrombolytic agents can produce placental haemorrhage and subsequent prematurity and fetal loss.

eptotermin alfa.statement: Altering the normal levels of bone morphogenic proteins, including this protein in the embryo may alter the normal course of action of these agents, and possibly result in fetal malformation.

ergometrine.statement: This drug induces uterine contraction and may cause premature or hypertonic labour. Products containing ergometrine should be avoided during pregnancy.

ergotamine.statement: Standard oral dose regimens for migraine headaches in the first half of pregnancy do not appear to pose hazards to the fetus. Ergotamine induces uterine contraction and may therefore cause premature parturition or hypertonic labour. Larger doses or more frequent use may jeopardise the fetus because of the potential for impeding fetal blood supply.

eribulin.statement: Eribulin is embryotoxic, fetotoxic and teratogenic in rats at less than the recommended human dose based on body surface area.

erlotinib.statement: This drug inhibits EGFR. EGFR plays an important role in embryofetal development.

ertugliflozin pyroglutamic acid.statement: Based on results from animal studies, ertugliflozin may affect renal development and maturation. Use in during pregnancy is not recommended.

ertugliflozin pyroglutamic acid; metform.statement: Based on results from animal studies, ertugliflozin may affect renal development and maturation. No animal embryofetal development studies have been performed with ertugliflozin and metformin in combination. Use in pregnancy is not recommended.

ertugliflozin pyroglutamic acid; sitagli.statement: Based on results from animal studies, ertugliflozin may affect renal development and maturation. No animal embryofetal development studies have been performed with ertugliflozin and sitagliptin in combination. Use in pregnancy is not recommended.

escitalopram.statement: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) have had limited use in pregnancy without a reported increase in birth defects.  The use of SSRIs in the third trimester may result in a withdrawal state in the newborn infant.

eslicarbazepine acetate.statement: Embyrofetal effects have been observed in animals including increases in pre-and post-implantation loss, decreases in fetal weight, an increased incidence of major and minor malformations and variations, at subclinical doses. Effects include minor and major rib abnormalities, cleft palate, missing thoracic neural arches and offset or hemicentric thoracic centra in mice, pelvic cavitation of the kidney, missing caudal vertebra, ossification delays and ridged palate.

esmolol.statement: Beta-adrenergic blocking agents may cause pharmacological effects such as bradycardia in the fetus and newborn infant.

estramustine.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

etacrynic acid.statement: Thiazides, related diuretics and loop diuretics may cause electrolyte disturbances in the fetus. Neonatal thrombocytopenia has been reported with thiazides and related diuretics. Loop diuretics, like frusemide and bumetanide, are probably also associated with this risk. During the latter part of pregnancy products of this type should only be given on sound indications, and then in the lowest effective dose.

etanercept.statement: Etanercept crosses the placenta and has been detected in the serum of  infants born to female patients treated with etanercept. Infants may be at increased risk of infection.

ethosuximide.statement: Compared to conventional anticonvulsants, the extent of the risk of this drug is unknown.

etoposide.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

etoricoxib.statement:
Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory (NSAIDs) agents inhibit prostaglandin synthesis which may adversely affect pregnancy. Refer to the relevant texts in the Product Information of individual NSAID products for information regarding the suitability for use in pregnancy. 

Epidemiological studies suggest an increased risk of spontaneous abortion after use of prostaglandin synthesis inhibitors in early pregnancy.

When given during the latter part of pregnancy, may cause closure of the fetal ductus arteriosus, oligohydramnios, fetal renal impairment, inhibition of platelet aggregation, and delay labour and birth.

Continuous treatment with NSAIDs during the last trimester of pregnancy should only be given on sound indications. During the last few days before expected birth, agents with an inhibitory effect on prostaglandin synthesis should be avoided.

etrasimod arginine.statement: Administration of etrasimod during pregnancy in rats and rabbits produced adverse effects on development, including embryolethality, fetal malformations and an increased number of stillbirths.

etretinate.statement: This drug is teratogenic at doses within the therapeutic range. The drug is stored in the body for several months after cessation. Because of the long half-life of this drug and storage in fat, patients are advised not to conceive until two years after cessation of treatment because of risk of birth defects. Should pregnancy occur during treatment with this drug, there is a high risk of birth defects.

exemestane.statement: Exemestane disrupts oestrogen dependent metabolism and may result in abortion.

ezetimibe.statement: The physiological hyperlipidaemia of pregnancy does not require treatment.

factor ix (human).statement: The safe use of this drug during pregnancy has not been established in controlled clinical trials.

faricimab.statement: Based on the mechanism of action of faricimab (VEGF and Ang-2 inhibition), there is a potential risk to female reproductive capacity, and to embryo-fetal development. VEGF inhibition has been shown to cause malformations, embryofetal resorption and decreased fetal weight. No dedicated studies addressing the effects of Ang-2 inhibition on pregnancy are available. Based on nonclinical information Ang-2 inhibition may lead to effects comparable to VEGF inhibition. Systemic exposures after ocular administration of faricimab is very low.

felodipine.statement: Calcium channel blockers carry the potential to produce fetal hypoxia associated with maternal hypotension.

fenfluramine.statement: Weight reduction using appetite suppressant drugs is not recommended in pregnancy.

fenofibrate.statement: The physiological hyperlipidaemia of pregnancy does not require treatment.

fentanyl.statement: Opioid analgesics may cause respiratory depression in the newborn infant. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

fentanyl / ropivacaine.statement: Opioid analgesics may cause respiratory depression in the newborn infant. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

finasteride.statement: Finasteride may cause abnormalities of the external genitalia of a male fetus.

finerenone.statement: Adverse effects on embryofetal development, including teratogenicity, were observed with finerenone in rats.

fingolimod.statement: This drug causes cardiovascular malformations in rats, and post-implantation loss and/or abortion in rats and rabbits.

fluconazole.statement: Effective contraceptive measures should be considered in women of child-bearing potential and should continue throughout the treatment period and for approximately 1 week (5 to 6 half-lives) after the final dose. There have been reports of spontaneous abortion and congenital abnormalities in infants whose mothers were treated with 150 mg of fluconazole as a single or repeated dose in the first trimester. Repeated doses of fluconazole (400-800mg daily) have been associated with a consistent pattern of birth defects similar to those seen in animal studies.

fludarabine.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

flunisolide.statement: The benefits of asthma control outweigh any potential for an adverse pregnancy outcome.

flunitrazepam.statement: Benzodiazepines may cause hypotonia, respiratory depression and hypothermia in the newborn infant if used in high doses during labour. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

fluorouracil.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

fluoxetine.statement: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) have had limited use in pregnancy without a reported increase in birth defects.  The use of SSRIs in the third trimester may result in a withdrawal state in the newborn infant.

fluoxymesterone.statement: Anabolic steroids and other substances with androgenic effects may have a virilising effect on the female fetus and should be avoided during pregnancy.

flupenthixol.statement: When given in high doses during late pregnancy, related compounds have caused prolonged neurological disturbances in the newborn infant.

fluphenazine.statement: When given in high doses during late pregnancy, phenothiazines have caused prolonged neurological disturbances in the newborn infant.

flurazepam.statement: Benzodiazepines may cause hypotonia, respiratory depression and hypothermia in the newborn infant if used in high doses during labour. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

flurbiprofen.statement: The safety of this product for use in human pregnancy has not been established. Reproductive studies in rats showed that flurbiprofen had a significant effect on parturition at doses of >0.4 mg/kg/day. Additionally, flurbiprofen was considered embryolethal at doses > 4.0 mg/kg/day. Because of the known effects of prostaglandin-inhibiting drugs on the fetal cardiovascular system of rats (closure of the ductus arteriosus), the use of flurbiprofen eye drops during late pregnancy should be avoided. Flurbiprofen should only be used in pregnancy when the potential benefit to the patient outweighs the potential risk to the fetus.

fluticasone furoate.statement: The benefits of asthma control outweigh any potential for an adverse pregnancy outcome.

fluticasone propionate.statement: The benefits of asthma control outweigh any potential for an adverse pregnancy outcome.

fluvastatin.statement: Cholesterol and other products of the cholesterol biosynthesis pathway are essential components for fetal development, including synthesis of steroids and cell membranes. Because of the ability of inhibitors of HMG-CoA reductase to decrease the synthesis of cholesterol and possibly other products of the cholesterol biosynthesis pathway, these drugs may cause fetal harm when administered to a pregnant woman. The physiological hyperlipidaemia of pregnancy does not require treatment.

fluvoxamine.statement: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) have had limited use in pregnancy without a reported increase in birth defects.  The use of SSRIs in the third trimester may result in a withdrawal state in the newborn infant.

fondaparinux.statement: All anticoagulants and thrombolytic agents can produce placental haemorrhage and subsequent prematurity and fetal loss.

fosinopril.statement: When taken during the second and third trimesters, ACE inhibitors cause a range of abnormalities including renal dysfunction and oligohydramnios. These can be associated with fetal death in utero. Although no adverse fetal effects have been linked to first trimester drug use of ACE inhibitors, the number of exposures reported is too small to determine conclusively that ACE inhibitors are safe in the first trimester.  Pregnant women who are taking ACE inhibitors should be changed as quickly as possible to other antihypertensive medication to maintain normal blood pressure.  It is generally advisable not to use ACE inhibitors for the management of hypertension in women who are likely to become pregnant.

fotemustine.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

framycetin.statement: There is evidence of selective uptake of aminoglycosides by the fetal kidney resulting in damage (probably reversible) to immature nephrons. Eighth cranial nerve damage has also been reported following in utero exposure to some of the aminoglycosides. Because of their chemical similarity, all aminoglycosides must be considered potentially nephrotoxic and ototoxic to the fetus. It should also be noted that therapeutic blood concentrations in the mother do not equate with safety for the fetus.

frusemide (furosemide).statement: Thiazides, related diuretics and loop diuretics may cause electrolyte disturbances in the fetus. Neonatal thrombocytopenia has been reported with thiazides and related diuretics. Loop diuretics, like frusemide and bumetanide, are probably also associated with this risk. During the latter part of pregnancy products of this type should only be given on sound indications, and then in the lowest effective dose.

fulvestrant.statement: This drug is teratogenic in rats. It may cause fetal harm when administered to a pregnant woman.

furosemide.statement: Thiazides, related diuretics and loop diuretics may cause electrolyte disturbances in the fetus. Neonatal thrombocytopenia has been reported with thiazides and related diuretics. Loop diuretics, like frusemide and bumetanide, are probably also associated with this risk. During the latter part of pregnancy products of this type should only be given on sound indications, and then in the lowest effective dose.

fusidic acid.statement: This drug may cause kernicterus in babies during the first month of life by displacing bilirubin from plasma albumin. Fusidic acid should be avoided if possible during the last month of pregnancy.

gabapentin.statement: Compared to conventional anticonvulsants, the extent of the risk of this drug is unknown.

gallamine.statement: There have been no demonstrated adverse effects in the fetus or the newborn infant.

ganciclovir.statement: Ganciclovir has been shown to be teratogenic and embryotoxic in animals.

ganirelix.statement: There is a theoretical risk of abortion if gonadotrophin releasing hormone antagonists are used during pregnancy.

gefitinib.statement: This drug inhibits EGFR. EGFR plays an important role in embryofetal development.

gemcitabine.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

gemfibrozil.statement: The physiological hyperlipidaemia of pregnancy does not require treatment.

gemtuzumab ozogamicin.statement: Gemtuzumab ozogamicin may cause embryofetal lethality and malformations based on findings in anmials

gentamicin.statement: There is evidence of selective uptake of aminoglycosides by the fetal kidney resulting in damage (probably reversible) to immature nephrons. Eighth cranial nerve damage has also been reported following in utero exposure to some of the aminoglycosides. Because of their chemical similarity, all aminoglycosides must be considered potentially nephrotoxic and ototoxic to the fetus. It should also be noted that therapeutic blood concentrations in the mother do not equate with safety for the fetus.

gestoden.statement: If taken by the mother at or after 8 weeks post conception, these drugs can cause virilisation of the female fetus. This is a dose-dependent effect. Prior to 8 weeks post conception, they have no virilising effects.

gestrinone.statement: This drug may interfere with pregnancy and in animal tests caused masculinisation of female fetuses. Gestrinone may not inhibit ovulation in all women.

glibenclamide.statement: Sulphonylureas may enter the fetal circulation and may cause neonatal hypoglycaemia.

glibenclamide / metformin.statement: Sulphonylureas may enter the fetal circulation and may cause neonatal hypoglycaemia.

gliclazide.statement: Sulphonylureas may enter the fetal circulation and may cause neonatal hypoglycaemia.

glimepiride.statement: Sulphonylureas may enter the fetal circulation and may cause neonatal hypoglycaemia.

glipizide.statement: Sulphonylureas may enter the fetal circulation and may cause neonatal hypoglycaemia.

glofitamab.statement: Based on its mechanism of action, glofitamab is likely to cause fetal B-cell depletion when administered to a pregnant woman, posing a risk of opportunistic infections in the neonate. Glofitamab-induced cytokine release may also pose a risk for embryofetal loss.

golimumab.statement: IgG antibodies are known to cross the placental barrier. This drug has the potential to affect embryofetal survival due to its pharmacological action.

goserelin.statement: There is a theoretical risk of abortion or fetal abnormality if GnRH agonists are used during pregnancy.

haloperidol.statement: When given in high doses during late pregnancy, butyrophenones may cause prolonged neurological disturbances in the newborn infant.

halothane.statement: All general anaesthetics carry the potential to produce central nervous system and respiratory depression in the newborn infant. In routine practice this does not appear to be a problem. However, in the compromised fetus, careful consideration should be given to this potential depression and to the selection of particular anaesthetic drugs, doses and techniques.

heparin.statement: All anticoagulants and thrombolytic agents can produce placental haemorrhage and subsequent prematurity and fetal loss.

hydralazine.statement: Following intravenous administration, hydralazine has been associated with fetal distress and fetal arrhythmia in the last trimester of pregnancy.

hydrochlorothiazide.statement: Thiazides, related diuretics and loop diuretics may cause electrolyte disturbances in the fetus. Neonatal thrombocytopenia has been reported with thiazides and related diuretics. Loop diuretics, like frusemide and bumetanide, are probably also associated with this risk. During the latter part of pregnancy products of this type should only be given on sound indications, and then in the lowest effective dose.

hydromorphone.statement: Opioid analgesics may cause respiratory depression in the newborn infant. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

hydroxycarbamide.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

hydroxychloroquine.statement: When used in high doses and for prolonged periods, chloroquine and related substances may cause neurological disturbances and interference with hearing, balance and vision in the fetus.

hydroxychloroquine (antimalarial).statement: When used in high doses and for prolonged periods, chloroquine and related substances may cause neurological disturbances and interference with hearing, balance and vision in the fetus. The use of this drug in the treatment of malaria is accepted because the small risk to the fetus is outweighed by the benefits to the mother and fetus.  Prophylaxis in high risk situations is also justified.

hydroxyprogesterone.statement: If taken by the mother at or after 8 weeks post conception, these drugs can cause virilisation of the female fetus. This is a dose-dependent effect. Prior to 8 weeks post conception, they have no virilising effects.

hydroxyurea (hydroxycarbamide).statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

ibrutinib.statement: This drug is embryotoxic in both pregnant rats and rabbits, and teratogenic when administered to pregnant animals.

ibuprofen.statement:
Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory (NSAIDs) agents inhibit prostaglandin synthesis which may adversely affect pregnancy. Refer to the relevant texts in the Product Information of individual NSAID products for information regarding the suitability for use in pregnancy. 

Epidemiological studies suggest an increased risk of spontaneous abortion after use of prostaglandin synthesis inhibitors in early pregnancy.

When given during the latter part of pregnancy, may cause closure of the fetal ductus arteriosus, oligohydramnios, fetal renal impairment, inhibition of platelet aggregation, and delay labour and birth.

Continuous treatment with NSAIDs during the last trimester of pregnancy should only be given on sound indications. During the last few days before expected birth, agents with an inhibitory effect on prostaglandin synthesis should be avoided.

idarubicin.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

idelalisib.statement: This drug is embryotoxic in both pregnant rats and rabbits, and teratogenic when administered to pregnant animals.

ifosfamide.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

imatinib.statement: This drug is embryotoxic in both pregnant rats and rabbits, and teratogenic when administered to pregnant animals.

imipramine.statement: Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged maternal use of tricyclic antidepressants.

indapamide.statement: Thiazides, related diuretics and loop diuretics may cause electrolyte disturbances in the fetus. Neonatal thrombocytopenia has been reported with thiazides and related diuretics. Loop diuretics, like frusemide and bumetanide, are probably also associated with this risk. During the latter part of pregnancy products of this type should only be given on sound indications, and then in the lowest effective dose.
