infigratinib.statement: This drug is embryotoxic and fetotoxic in both pregnant rats and rabbits, and teratogenic when administered to pregnant animals.

infliximab.statement: Infliximab crosses the placenta. Infliximab has been detected in the serum of infants up to six months following birth. After in utero exposure to infliximab, infants may be at increased risk of infection.

influenza vaccine, inactivated (seasonal.category: A or B2  (depending on brand of vaccine)

inotuzumab ozogamicin.statement: IgG antibodies are known to cross the placental barrier. This drug has the potential to affect embryofetal survival due to its pharmacological action.

interferon beta-1a.statement: Interferon beta-1a has abortifacient activity in monkeys.

interferon beta-1b.statement: This drug has abortifacient activity in monkeys. Spontaneous abortions have been reported in subjects with multiple sclerosis in controlled clinical trials.

iodoform with bismuth subnitrate.statement: Sufficient iodine may be absorbed to affect fetal thyroid development and function.

ipilimumab.statement: IgG antibodies are known to cross the placental barrier. This drug may affect embryofetal survival due to its pharmacological action.

irbesartan.statement: When used in pregnancy during the second and third trimesters, drugs that act directly on the renin-angiotensin system can cause injury and even death in the developing fetus. Although no adverse fetal effects have been linked to first trimester drug use of ARAs, the number of exposures reported is too small to determine conclusively that ARAs are safe in the first trimester. Pregnant women who are taking ARAs should be changed as quickly as possible to other antihypertensive medication to maintain normal blood pressure. It is generally advisable not to use ARAs for the management of hypertension in women who are likely to become pregnant.

irinotecan.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

irinotecan (nanoliposomal as sucrosofate.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

isavuconazole.statement: Isavuconazole must not be used during pregnancy except in patients with severe or potentially life-threatening fungal infections, in whom isavuconazole may be used if the anticipated benefits outweigh the possible risks to the fetus. Isavuconazonium was associated with increases in the incidences of rudimentary cervical ribs in rats and rabbits at doses equivalent to about one fifth and one tenth of the clinical exposures.  In rats, increases in the incidences of zygomatic arch fusion and supernumerary ribs/rudimentary supernumerary ribs were also noted at doses equivalent to one fifth the clinical dose. The potential risk for humans is unknown.

isoflurane.statement: All general anaesthetics carry the potential to produce central nervous system and respiratory depression in the newborn infant. In routine practice this does not appear to be a problem. However, in the compromised fetus, careful consideration should be given to this potential depression and to the selection of particular anaesthetic drugs, doses and techniques.

isotretinoin (systemic).statement: Isotretinoin is teratogenic and must not be used by women who are pregnant or who may possibly become pregnant while undergoing treatment and for one month after isotretinoin has stopped. Should pregnancy occur during treatment with this drug, there is a high risk of birth defects. (Refer to current Product Information).

isotretinoin (topical).statement: Isotretinoin is known to be teratogenic when administered orally in human beings. It is associated with major birth defects and with a small risk of spontaneous abortion.

isoxsuprine.statement: Maternal isoxsuprine administration for prevention of premature labour has been associated with tachycardia, hypoglycaemia, hypocalcaemia, ileus and hypotension in the neonate.

isradipine.statement: Calcium channel blockers carry the potential to produce fetal hypoxia associated with maternal hypotension.

ivabradine.statement: Animal studies have shown that this drug is embryotoxic and teratogenic (cardiac defects in rats and ectrodactyly in rabbits). Increased post-natal mortality and enlarged hearts were seen in rat pups from dams treated with this drug.

ixazomib.statement: If sufficient placental transfer occurred, ixazomib has the potential to disrupt protein cycling in the developing fetus and, therefore, may have an adverse effect on fetal development.

ixekizumab.statement: The mechanism of action of ixekizumab suggests a theoretical risk that its use during pregnancy may affect neonatal immunity.

japanese encephalitis vaccine (live, att.statement: Currently available live virus vaccines have not caused teratogenic effects in humans. The ATAGI publication, “The Australian Immunisation Handbook”, should be consulted for more comprehensive information.

kanamycin.statement: There is evidence of selective uptake of aminoglycosides by the fetal kidney resulting in damage (probably reversible) to immature nephrons. Eighth cranial nerve damage has also been reported following in utero exposure to some of the aminoglycosides. Because of their chemical similarity, all aminoglycosides must be considered potentially nephrotoxic and ototoxic to the fetus. It should also be noted that therapeutic blood concentrations in the mother do not equate with safety for the fetus.

ketamine.statement: All general anaesthetics carry the potential to produce central nervous system and respiratory depression in the newborn infant. In routine practice this does not appear to be a problem. However, in the compromised fetus, careful consideration should be given to this potential depression and to the selection of particular anaesthetic drugs, doses and techniques.

Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory (NSAIDs) agents inhibit prostaglandin synthesis which may adversely affect pregnancy. Refer to the relevant texts in the Product Information of individual NSAID products for information regarding the suitability for use in pregnancy.

When given during the latter part of pregnancy, may cause closure of the fetal ductus arteriosus, oligohydramnios, fetal renal impairment, inhibition of platelet aggregation, and delay labour and birth.

Continuous treatment with NSAIDs during the last trimester of pregnancy should only be given on sound indications. During the last few days before expected birth, agents with an inhibitory effect on prostaglandin synthesis should be avoided.

ketorolac.statement:
Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory (NSAIDs) agents inhibit prostaglandin synthesis which may adversely affect pregnancy. Refer to the relevant texts in the Product Information of individual NSAID products for information regarding the suitability for use in pregnancy.

When given during the latter part of pregnancy, may cause closure of the fetal ductus arteriosus, oligohydramnios, fetal renal impairment, inhibition of platelet aggregation, and delay labour and birth.

Continuous treatment with NSAIDs during the last trimester of pregnancy should only be given on sound indications. During the last few days before expected birth, agents with an inhibitory effect on prostaglandin synthesis should be avoided.

labetalol.statement: Beta-adrenergic blocking agents may cause pharmacological effects such as bradycardia in the fetus and newborn infant.

lamotrigine.statement: Compared to conventional anticonvulsants, the extent of the risk of this drug is unknown.

landiolol.statement: Based on the pharmacological action of Beta-adrenergic blocking agents, landiolol may cause harm including  bradycardia in the fetus and newborn infant when administered to pregnant women.

lanreotide.statement: This drug may cause fetal growth retardation, probably due to suppression of growth hormone.

lapatinib.statement: This drug inhibits EGFR. EGFR plays an important role in embryofetal development.

larotrectinib.statement: Based on its mechanism of action and findings in animal studies, larotrectinib may cause fetal malformations.

latanoprost / timolol.statement: Beta-adrenergic blocking agents may cause pharmacological effects such as bradycardia in the fetus and newborn infant.

ledipasvir / sofosbuvir with ribavirin.statement: Although there are no pertinent human data, ribavirin has been found to be teratogenic and/or embryolethal in nearly all species in which it has been tested. Malformations of skull, palate, eye, jaw, skeleton and gastrointestinal tract were noted in animal studies. Survival of fetuses and offspring was reduced.

leflunomide.statement: This drug may cause fetal harm in humans. This drug must not be given to pregnant women, or women of childbearing potential who are not using reliable contraception during treatment with this drug and for a certain period of time thereafter. Pregnancy must be excluded before the start of treatment with this drug.

lenalidomide.statement: This drug is structurally related to thalidomide. Thalidomide is a known human teratogen that causes severe life threatening human birth defects. If lenalidomide is taken during pregnancy, it may cause birth defects or death to an unborn baby. Women should be advised to avoid pregnancy while taking lenalidomide and for four weeks after stopping the drug.

lenvatinib.statement: Angiogenesis plays a key role in embryo-fetal development. VEGF receptor inhibitors inhibit angiogenesis and can cause fetal harm when administered to pregnant women.

lepirudin.statement: All anticoagulants and thrombolytic agents can produce placental haemorrhage and subsequent prematurity and fetal loss.

lercanidipine.statement: Calcium channel blockers carry the potential to produce fetal hypoxia associated with maternal hypotension.

letrozole.statement: This drug disrupts oestrogen dependant metabolism and is associated with fetal malformation and mortality in animals.

leuprorelin.statement: There is a theoretical risk of abortion or fetal abnormality if GnRH agonists are used during pregnancy.

levetiracetam.statement: Compared to conventional anticonvulsants, the extent of the risk of this drug is unknown.

levobunolol.statement: Beta-adrenergic blocking agents may cause pharmacological effects such as bradycardia in the fetus and newborn infant.

levobunolol (ophthalmic).statement: Beta-adrenergic blocking agents may cause pharmacological effects such as bradycardia in the fetus and newborn infant.

levocabastine.statement: Inadvertent short term exposure during the first trimester is unlikely to cause a hazard to the fetus, but it has been shown to be teratogenic in two species of animals and until human data are available, it should be suspected of being teratogenic.

linagliptin / metformin.statement: It is important to achieve strict normoglycaemia during pregnancy.

lindane.statement: Lindane penetrates human skin and has been reported to cause signs of CNS irritation. Because of this toxic potential it is preferable, whenever possible, to use other medications during pregnancy.

lisinopril.statement: When taken during the second and third trimesters, ACE inhibitors cause a range of abnormalities including renal dysfunction and oligohydramnios. These can be associated with fetal death in utero. Although no adverse fetal effects have been linked to first trimester drug use of ACE inhibitors, the number of exposures reported is too small to determine conclusively that ACE inhibitors are safe in the first trimester.  Pregnant women who are taking ACE inhibitors should be changed as quickly as possible to other antihypertensive medication to maintain normal blood pressure.  It is generally advisable not to use ACE inhibitors for the management of hypertension in women who are likely to become pregnant.

lithium salts.statement: The risk of birth defects may be increased when lithium is used during the first trimester. Second trimester detailed ultrasound examination and fetal echocardiography should be considered for women who have been treated with lithium during the first trimester of pregnancy. The newborn may show signs of lithium toxicity.

lomustine.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

lorazepam.statement: Benzodiazepines may cause hypotonia, respiratory depression and hypothermia in the newborn infant if used in high doses during labour. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

lorlatinib.statement: Based on its mechanism of action and findings in animal studies, lorlatinib may cause embryo-fetal harm (including embryolethality, abortions and malformations).

losartan.statement: When used in pregnancy during the second and third trimesters, drugs that act directly on the renin-angiotensin system can cause injury and even death in the developing fetus. Although no adverse fetal effects have been linked to first trimester drug use of ARAs, the number of exposures reported is too small to determine conclusively that ARAs are safe in the first trimester. Pregnant women who are taking ARAs should be changed as quickly as possible to other antihypertensive medication to maintain normal blood pressure. lurbinectedin.statement: This drug is embryotoxic and fetotoxic when administered to pregnant rats.

lutetium (177lu).statement: Teratogenicity is a class effect of radioactive agents, which induce multiple fetal abnormalities. Radioactive drugs are likely to cause major birth defects if used during pregnancy.

macitentan.statement: Teratogenicity is a class effect of these agents, which induce multiple fetal abnormalities in animal studies. These drugs are likely to cause major birth defects if used during pregnancy.

maribavir.statement: Maribavir has been shown to reduce embryo-fetal survival and delay offspring development in rats.

mazindol.statement: Weight reduction using appetite suppressant drugs is not recommended in pregnancy.

measles-mumps-rubella vaccine.statement: Women of child bearing age should be tested for rubella antibodies prior to pregnancy. All seronegative women, provided they are not pregnant, should be offered rubella vaccine. Those administering the vaccine should be careful to instruct women to whom it is given that they should not become pregnant for at least two full menstrual cycles because rubella vaccine can cause fetal infection. However, to date, there have not been any rubella-like birth defects in the live born infants (about 400) of seronegative mothers vaccinated during or just before pregnancy. Based on this experience, rubella vaccination during pregnancy need not be the reason to recommend interruption of pregnancy.

measles-mumps-rubella-varicella vaccine.statement: Women of child bearing age should be tested for rubella antibodies prior to pregnancy. All seronegative women, provided they are not pregnant, should be offered rubella vaccine. Those administering the vaccine should be careful to instruct women to whom it is given that they should not become pregnant for at least two full menstrual cycles because rubella vaccine can cause fetal infection. However, to date, there have not been any rubella-like birth defects in the live born infants (about 400) of seronegative mothers vaccinated during or just before pregnancy. medroxyprogesterone (oral and im high do.statement: May cause virilisation of fetus if taken at or after 8 weeks post conception.

melphalan.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

meningococcal serogroups a, c, w-135 and.category: B1 or B2 depending on brand

meprobamate.statement: This drug may cause hypotension, respiratory depression and hypothermia in the newborn infant.

mercaptopurine.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

mesalazine.statement: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs inhibit prostaglandin synthesis and, when given during the latter part of pregnancy, may cause closure of the fetal ductus arteriosus, fetal renal impairment, inhibition of platelet aggregation, and delay labour and birth. Continuous treatment with non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs during the last trimester of pregnancy should only be given on sound indications. During the last few days before expected birth, agents with an inhibitory effect on prostaglandin synthesis should be avoided.

mesterolone.statement: Anabolic steroids and other substances with androgenic effects may have a virilising effect on the female fetus and should be avoided during pregnancy. This drug is for use in male patients only. In animal studies, it inhibits spermatogenesis.

metaraminol.statement: This drug may cause fetal hypoxia by constricting the uterine vessels thereby limiting placental perfusion.

metformin.statement: It is important to achieve strict normoglycaemia during pregnancy.

methacycline.statement: Tetracyclines are safe for use during the first 18 weeks of pregnancy (16 weeks post conception) after which they cause discolouration of the baby’s teeth.

methadone.statement: Opioid analgesics may cause respiratory depression in the newborn infant. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

methenolone.statement: Anabolic steroids and other substances with androgenic effects may have a virilising effect on the female fetus and should be avoided during pregnancy.

methohexitone.statement: All general anaesthetics carry the potential to produce central nervous system and respiratory depression in the newborn infant. In routine practice this does not appear to be a problem. However, in the compromised fetus, careful consideration should be given to this potential depression and to the selection of particular anaesthetic drugs, doses and techniques.

methotrexate.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

methoxsalen (ecp extracorporeal photophe.statement: When given to rats during organogenesis, methoxsalen caused significant fetal toxicity (not for direct injection into patients; only for extracorporeal photopheresis  of leukocyte enriched blood in a photoactivation bag )

methoxyflurane.statement: All general anaesthetics carry the potential to produce central nervous system and respiratory depression in the newborn infant. In routine practice this does not appear to be a problem. However, in the compromised fetus, careful consideration should be given to this potential depression and to the selection of particular anaesthetic drugs, doses and techniques.

methsuximide.statement: Compared to conventional anticonvulsants, the extent of the risk of this drug is unknown.

methyclothiazide.statement: Thiazides, related diuretics and loop diuretics may cause electrolyte disturbances in the fetus. During the latter part of pregnancy products of this type should only be given on sound indications, and then in the lowest effective dose.

methyl aminolevulinate.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

methylene blue.statement: There have been reports of haemolytic anaemia and hyperbilirubinaemia in neonates exposed to methylene blue in the amniotic cavity.

methylphenidate.statement: Dosing in rabbits produced teratogenic effects at doses approximately 5 fold that in humans receiving the maximal recommended dose. Dosing to rats from early pregnancy until weaning reduced offspring weight and marginal alterations in neuromotor performance in offspring at doses approximately 3-6 fold the maximum recommended clinical dose on a mg/m2 basis. Dosing to juvenile male and female rats from weaning through mating, pregnancy and lactation until offspring weaning, was associated with reduced body weight gain and motor activity in males as well as reduced offspring weight and postnatal survival - at doses 1 to 3 fold that expected in adults or children given the maximum recommended clinical dose. The safety of methylphenidate for use during human pregnancy has not been established. Cases of fetal cardiac malformations have been identified in large observational studies. Methylphenidate should not be prescribed for pregnant women unless, in opinion of the physician, the potential benefits outweigh the possible risks

methyltestosterone.statement: Anabolic steroids and other substances with androgenic effects may have a virilising effect on the female fetus and should be avoided during pregnancy.

methysergide.statement: Standard oral dose regimens for migraine headaches in the first half of pregnancy do not appear to pose hazards to the fetus. Ergotamine induces uterine contraction and may therefore cause premature parturition or hypertonic labour. Larger doses or more frequent use may jeopardise the fetus because of the potential for impeding fetal blood supply.

metolazone.statement: Thiazides, related diuretics and loop diuretics may cause electrolyte disturbances in the fetus. Neonatal thrombocytopenia has been reported with thiazides and related diuretics. Loop diuretics, like frusemide and bumetanide, are probably also associated with this risk. During the latter part of pregnancy products of this type should only be given on sound indications, and then in the lowest effective dose.

metoprolol.statement: Beta-adrenergic blocking agents may cause pharmacological effects such as bradycardia in the fetus and newborn infant.

midazolam.statement: Benzodiazepines may cause hypotonia, respiratory depression and hypothermia in the newborn infant if used in high doses during labour. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

midodrine hydrochloride.statement: This drug has demonstrated embryofetal harm and lethality in animals.

midostaurin.statement: This drug is embryotoxic and embryolethal in pregnant animals.

miglustat.statement: This drug might be expected to cross the placenta and has shown to be embryotoxic in rats and rabbits.

minocycline.statement: Tetracyclines are safe for use during the first 18 weeks of pregnancy (16 weeks post conception) after which they cause discolouration of the baby’s teeth.

minoxidil.statement: This drug has been associated with hypertrichosis in the newborn infant following exposure in utero.

misoprostol.statement: This drug can produce serious birth defects. It also can cause miscarriage that could lead to potentially dangerous bleeding.

mitomycin.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

mitozantrone.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

mobocertinib succinate.statement: Based on its mechanism of action and data from animal studies (embryo-fetal death and decreased fetal weight in  pregnant rats treated during the period of organogenesis), mobocertinib can cause fetal harm when administered during pregnancy.

modafinil.statement: This drug is embryotoxic and fetotoxic when administered to pregnant rats and rabbits. It also decreased viability in the offspring when administered to pregnant rats.

mogamulizumab.statement: There are no data from the use in pregnant women. In pregnant cynomolgus monkeys, mogamulizumab did not cause malformations, embryofetal lethality or growth retardation. However, mogamulizumab was shown to cross the placental barrier, and this resulted in pharmacological activity in the foetuses, evident as a decrease in CCR4-expressing lymphocytes. Mogamulizumab is not recommended during pregnancy unless the potential benefit for the mother outweighs the potential risk to the foetus.

molnupiravir.statement: Based on animal data, molnupiravir may cause fetal harm when administered to pregnant women.

momelotinib.statement: Momelotinib is contraindicated in pregnancy. Malformations, embryofetal lethality and other adverse effects were observed with momelotinib in animals at clinically relevant exposure levels.

morphine.statement: Opioid analgesics may cause respiratory depression in the newborn infant. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

mustine.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

mycophenolate mofetil.statement: It inhibits nucleic acid synthesis and may cause fetal malformations/death. Congenital malformations, including ear malformations i.e. abnormally formed or absent external/middle ear, have been reported in children of patients exposed to mycophenolate mofetil in combination with other immunosuppressants during pregnancy.

mycophenolic acid.statement: It inhibits nucleic acid synthesis and may cause fetal malformations/death. Congenital malformations, including ear malformations i.e. abnormally formed or absent external/middle ear, have been reported in children of patients exposed to mycophenolate mofetil in combination with other immunosuppressants during pregnancy.

nadroparin.statement: All anticoagulants and thrombolytic agents can produce placental haemorrhage and subsequent prematurity and fetal loss.

nafarelin.statement: There is a theoretical risk of abortion or fetal abnormality if GnRH agonists are used during pregnancy.

nandrolone.statement: Anabolic steroids and other substances with androgenic effects may have a virilising effect on the female fetus and should be avoided during pregnancy.

Continuous treatment with NSAIDs during the last trimester of pregnancy should only be given on sound indications. During the last few days before expected birth, agents with an inhibitory effect on prostaglandin synthesis should be avoided.

nateglinide.statement: Sulphonylureas may enter the fetal circulation and may cause neonatal hypoglycaemia.

nebivolol.statement: Beta-adrenergic blocking agents may cause pharmacological effects such as bradycardia in the fetus and newborn infant.

nelarabine.statement: Animal data indicate that exposure during pregnancy will likely lead to malformations of the fetus.

neomycin.statement: There is evidence of selective uptake of aminoglycosides by the fetal kidney resulting in damage (probably reversible) to immature nephrons. Eighth cranial nerve damage has also been reported following in utero exposure to some of the aminoglycosides. Because of their chemical similarity, all aminoglycosides must be considered potentially nephrotoxic and ototoxic to the fetus. It should also be noted that therapeutic blood concentrations in the mother do not equate with safety for the fetus.

nepafenac.statement:

Epidemiological studies suggest an increased risk of spontaneous abortion after use of prostaglandin synthesis inhibitors in early pregnancy.

When given during the latter part of pregnancy, may cause closure of the fetal ductus arteriosus, oligohydramnios, fetal renal impairment, inhibition of platelet aggregation, and delay labour and birth.

Continuous treatment with NSAIDs during the last trimester of pregnancy should only be given on sound indications. During the last few days before expected birth, agents with an inhibitory effect on prostaglandin synthesis should be avoided.

neratinib.statement: Studies in animals have shown embryofetal lethality and fetal morphological anomalies.

neratinib maleate.statement: Studies in animals have shown embryofetal lethality and fetal abnormalities. The potential risk for humans is unknown. Neratinib is not recommended during pregnancy. If neratinib is used during pregnancy, or if the patient becomes pregnant while taking neratinib, the patient should be informed of the potential hazard to the fetus.

netilmicin.statement: There is evidence of selective uptake of aminoglycosides by the fetal kidney resulting in damage (probably reversible) to immature nephrons. Eighth cranial nerve damage has also been reported following in utero exposure to some of the aminoglycosides. Because of their chemical similarity, all aminoglycosides must be considered potentially nephrotoxic and ototoxic to the fetus. It should also be noted that therapeutic blood concentrations in the mother do not equate with safety for the fetus.

netupitant / palonosetron.statement: In animals there were findings of reduced fetal weight and fetal abnormalities of the limbs, paws and ribs. There was also cleft palate and eye abnormalities in animals.

nicardipine.statement: Calcium channel blockers carry the potential to produce fetal hypoxia associated with maternal hypotension.

nicotine.statement: The harmful effects of cigarette smoking on maternal and fetal health are clearly established. The specific effects of nicotine therapy on fetal development are unknown. Short-term exposure during the first trimester is unlikely to cause a hazard to the fetus.

nifedipine.statement: Calcium channel blockers carry the potential to produce fetal hypoxia associated with maternal hypotension. Acute pulmonary oedema has been observed when calcium channel blockers, among others nifedipine (primarily in IR formulation), have been used as a tocolytic agent during pregnancy, especially in cases of multiple pregnancy (twins or more), with the intravenous route and/or concomitant use of beta-2 agonists.

nilotinib.statement: This drug is embryotoxic and embryolethal in pregnant animals.

nimodipine.statement: Calcium channel blockers carry the potential to produce fetal hypoxia associated with maternal hypotension.

nintedanib esilate.statement: Angiogenesis plays a key role in embryo-fetal development. It is teratogenic in pregnant animals. VEGF receptor inhibitors inhibit angiogenesis and can cause fetal harm when administered to pregnant women.

niraparib.statement: Based on its mechanism of action, niraparib could cause embryonic or fetal harm, including embryo-lethal and teratogenic effects

nisoldipine.statement: Calcium channel blockers carry the potential to produce fetal hypoxia associated with maternal hypotension.

nitrazepam.statement: Benzodiazepines may cause hypotonia, respiratory depression and hypothermia in the newborn infant if used in high doses during labour. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

nitrofurantoin (short term therapy).statement: Caution should be exercised when administering nitrofurantoin at term because of the possibility of producing haemolytic anaemia in patients with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency and due to immature enzyme systems in the early neonatal period.

nitrous oxide.statement: All general anaesthetics carry the potential to produce central nervous system and respiratory depression in the newborn infant. In routine practice this does not appear to be a problem. However, in the compromised fetus, careful consideration should be given to this potential depression and to the selection of particular anaesthetic drugs, doses and techniques.

nivolumab.statement: PD-1/PD-L1 is involved in fetomaternal tolerance. Administration during pregnancy may cause fetal harm, including abortion or stillbirth.

norethisterone.statement: If taken by the mother at or after 8 weeks post conception, these drugs can cause virilisation of the female fetus. This is a dose-dependent effect. Prior to 8 weeks post conception, they have no virilising effects.

nortriptyline.statement: Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged maternal use of tricyclic antidepressants.

obinutuzumab.statement: IgG antibodies are known to cross the placental barrier. This drug may affect embryofetal survival due to its pharmacological action.

ocrelizumab.statement: IgG antibodies are known to cross the placental barrier. This drug has the potential to affect embryofetal survival due to its pharmacological action.

octreotide.statement: This drug may cause fetal growth retardation, probably due to suppression of growth hormone.

ofatumumab (rmc).statement: IgG antibodies are known to cross the placental barrier. This drug has the potential to affect embryofetal survival due to its pharmacological action.

olanzapine.statement: Neonates exposed to antipsychotic drugs during the third trimester of pregnancy are at risk of experiencing extrapyramidal neurological disturbances and/or withdrawal symptoms following delivery. There have been reports of agitation, hypertonia, hypotonia, tremor, somnolence, respiratory distress and feeding disorder in these neonates.

olaparib.statement: Drugs which have caused, are suspected to have caused or may be expected to cause, an increased incidence of human fetal malformations or irreversible damage. These drugs may also have adverse pharmacological effects. Accompanying texts should be consulted for further details.

olipudase alfa.statement: An increased incidence of a malformation (exencephaly) was observed when pregnant mice (but not rabbits) were treated with olipudase alfa. The relevance of this observation for humans is unknown. Olipudase alfa is not recommended during pregnancy and in women of childbearing potential not using effective contraception, unless the potential benefits to the mother outweigh the potential risks, including those to the fetus.

olmesartan.statement: When used in pregnancy during the second and third trimesters, drugs that act directly on the renin-angiotensin system can cause injury and even death in the developing fetus. Although no adverse fetal effects have been linked to first trimester drug use of ARAs, the number of exposures reported is too small to determine conclusively that ARAs are safe in the first trimester. Pregnant women who are taking ARAs should be changed as quickly as possible to other antihypertensive medication to maintain normal blood pressure. It is generally advisable not to use ARAs for the management of hypertension in women who are likely to become pregnant.

olsalazine.statement: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs inhibit prostaglandin synthesis and, when given during the latter part of pregnancy, may cause closure of the fetal ductus arteriosus, fetal renal impairment, inhibition of platelet aggregation, and delay labour and birth. Continuous treatment with non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs during the last trimester of pregnancy should only be given on sound indications. During the last few days before expected birth, agents with an inhibitory effect on prostaglandin synthesis should be avoided.

oral contraceptives, combined.statement: Accumulated evidence reports that inadvertent exposure to these agents in early pregnancy has not been associated with an increased risk of birth defects.

orlistat.statement: Weight reduction using appetite suppressant drugs is not recommended in pregnancy.

osilodrostat.statement: High doses of osilodrostat administered to pregnant rats during organogenesis increased embryonic and fetal deaths, decreased fetal weights and malformations.

osimertinib.statement: This drug inhibits EGFR. EGFR plays an important role in embryofetal development.

oxaliplatin.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

oxandrolone.statement: Anabolic steroids and other substances with androgenic effects may have a virilising effect on the female fetus and should be avoided during pregnancy.

oxazepam.statement: Benzodiazepines may cause hypotonia, respiratory depression and hypothermia in the newborn infant if used in high doses during labour. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

oxprenolol.statement: Beta-adrenergic blocking agents may cause pharmacological effects such as bradycardia in the fetus and newborn infant.

oxycodone.statement: Opioid analgesics may cause respiratory depression in the newborn infant. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

oxycodone / naloxone.statement: Opioid analgesics may cause respiratory depression in the newborn infant. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

oxymetholone.statement: Anabolic steroids and other substances with androgenic effects may have a virilising effect on the female fetus and should be avoided during pregnancy.

oxytetracycline.statement: Tetracyclines are safe for use during the first 18 weeks of pregnancy (16 weeks post conception) after which they cause discolouration of the baby’s teeth.

oxytocin.statement: There have been instances of idiosyncratic sensitivity of the uterus resulting in fetal anoxia.

ozanimod.statement: This drug causes cardiovascular malformations, and post-implantation loss in rats and rabbits, consistent with its pharmacological mechanism of action.

paclitaxel.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

palbociclib.statement: Based on findings in animals and mechanism of action, palbociclib can cause fetal harm when administered to a pregnant woman.

paliperidone.statement: Neonates exposed to antipsychotic drugs during the third trimester of pregnancy are at risk of experiencing extrapyramidal neurological disturbances and/or withdrawal symptoms following delivery. There have been reports of agitation, hypertonia, hypotonia, tremor, somnolence, respiratory distress and feeding disorder in these neonates.

palovarotene.statement: Palovarotene is contraindicated in pregnancy due to the potential of severe and life-threatening birth defects. Palovarotene was shown to produce fetal malformations typical of retinoids (e.g., cleft palate, malformed skull bone, shortening of the long bones) at subclinical exposure levels in rats.

panitumumab.statement: IgG antibodies are known to cross the placental barrier. This drug may affect embryofetal survival due to its pharmacological action.

panobinostat.statement: Drugs which have caused, are suspected to have caused or may be expected to cause, an increased incidence of human fetal malformations or irreversible damage. These drugs may also have adverse pharmacological effects. Accompanying texts should be consulted for further details.

papaveretum.statement: Opioid analgesics may cause respiratory depression in the newborn infant. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

paracetomol / tramadol.statement: Opioid analgesics may cause respiratory depression in the newborn infant. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

paraldehyde.statement: This drug readily diffuses across the placenta and has been known to cause respiratory depression in neonates.

parecoxib.statement:
Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory (NSAIDs) agents inhibit prostaglandin synthesis which may adversely affect pregnancy. Refer to the relevant texts in the Product Information of individual NSAID products for information regarding the suitability for use in pregnancy. 

Epidemiological studies suggest an increased risk of spontaneous abortion after use of prostaglandin synthesis inhibitors in early pregnancy.

When given during the latter part of pregnancy, may cause closure of the fetal ductus arteriosus, oligohydramnios, fetal renal impairment, inhibition of platelet aggregation, and delay labour and birth.

Continuous treatment with NSAIDs during the last trimester of pregnancy should only be given on sound indications. During the last few days before expected birth, agents with an inhibitory effect on prostaglandin synthesis should be avoided.

paritaprevir / ritonavir / ombitasvir wi.statement: Although there are no pertinent human data, ribavirin has been found to be teratogenic and/or embryolethal in nearly all species in which it has been tested. Malformations of skull, palate, eye, jaw, skeleton and gastrointestinal tract were noted in animal studies. Survival of fetuses and offspring was reduced.

paritaprevir/ritonavir/ombitasvir/dasabu.statement: Although there are no pertinent human data, ribavirin has been found to be teratogenic and/or embryolethal in nearly all species in which it has been tested. Malformations of skull, palate, eye, jaw, skeleton and gastrointestinal tract were noted in animal studies. Survival of fetuses and offspring was reduced.

paroxetine.statement: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) have had limited use in pregnancy without a reported increase in birth defects.  The use of SSRIs in the third trimester may result in a withdrawal state in the newborn infant.

patisiran.statement: Patisiran administered to pregnant rabbits during the period of organogenesis resulted in embryofetal mortality and reduced fetal body weight at doses.

pazopanib.statement: Angiogenesis plays a key role in embryo-fetal development. It is teratogenic in pregnant animals. VEGF receptor inhibitors inhibit angiogenesis and can cause fetal harm when administered to pregnant women.

pegaspargase.statement: Cytotoxicity from asparaginase activity poses a risk for spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

peginterferon beta-1a (rch).statement: Peginterferon beta-1a has not been tested for reproductive toxicity in pregnant animals. Non-pegylated interferon beta-1a has shown no evidence of teratogenicity in pregnant animals, but has abortifacient activity in monkeys.

pegvaliase.statement: Animal studies have shown maternal reproductive toxicity that may have been associated with decreased blood phenylalanine concentrations below normal levels. Embryofetal lethality, lower fetal weights and multiple fetal malformations and/or variations were seen when administered to pregnant rabbits at maternotoxic doses. Maternal blood phenylalanine levels must be strictly controlled during pregnancy.

pembrolizumab.statement: PD-1/PD-L1 is involved in fetomaternal tolerance. Administration during pregnancy may cause fetal harm, including abortion or stillbirth.

pemetrexed.statement: Cytotoxic agents can produce spontaneous abortion, fetal loss and birth defects.

pemigatinib.statement: Based on its mechanism of action and data from animal studies, P pemigatinib can cause reproductive toxicity (post-implantation loss, malformations and reduced fetal weight) when administered during pregnancy.

penicillamine.statement: This drug can cause cutis laxa in the human fetus.

pentazocine.statement: Opioid analgesics may cause respiratory depression in the newborn infant. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

pentobarbitone.statement: Barbiturates can give rise to hypotension, respiratory depression and hypothermia in the newborn infant. Continuous treatment during pregnancy and administration during labour should be avoided.

pergolide.statement: Studies in rodents have shown no evidence of harm to the fetus. There are no adequate and well-controlled studies in pregnant women.  This drug should be used during pregnancy only if clearly needed.

periciazine.statement: When given in high doses during late pregnancy, phenothiazines have caused prolonged neurological disturbances in the newborn infant.

perindopril.statement: When taken during the second and third trimesters, ACE inhibitors cause a range of abnormalities including renal dysfunction and oligohydramnios. These can be associated with fetal death in utero. Although no adverse fetal effects have been linked to first trimester drug use of ACE inhibitors, the number of exposures reported is too small to determine conclusively that ACE inhibitors are safe in the first trimester.  Pregnant women who are taking ACE inhibitors should be changed as quickly as possible to other antihypertensive medication to maintain normal blood pressure.  It is generally advisable not to use ACE inhibitors for the management of hypertension in women who are likely to become pregnant.

perphenazine.statement: When given in high doses during late pregnancy, phenothiazines have caused prolonged neurological disturbances in the newborn infant.

pertuzumab.statement: IgG antibodies are known to cross the placental barrier. This drug has the potential to affect embryofetal survival due to its pharmacological action.

pethidine.statement: Opioid analgesics may cause respiratory depression in the newborn infant. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

phenindione.statement: This drug can cause birth defects when used in the first trimester of pregnancy. Use of phenindione should also be avoided during the third trimester of pregnancy as delivery during phenindione therapy may lead to excessive bleeding in the infant or the mother. All anticoagulants and thrombolytic agents can produce placental haemorrhage and subsequent prematurity and fetal loss.

phenoperidine.statement: Opioid analgesics may cause respiratory depression in the newborn infant. Withdrawal symptoms in newborn infants have been reported with prolonged use of this class of drugs.

phenoxybenzamine.statement: This drug is known to be mutagenic and carcinogenic in rodents.

phensuximide.statement: Compared to conventional anticonvulsants, the extent of the risk of this drug is unknown.

phentermine.statement: Weight reduction using appetite suppressant drugs is not recommended in pregnancy.

phenylbutazone.statement:
Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory (NSAIDs) agents inhibit prostaglandin synthesis which may adversely affect pregnancy. Refer to the relevant texts in the Product Information of individual NSAID products for information regarding the suitability for use in pregnancy. 

Epidemiological studies suggest an increased risk of spontaneous abortion after use of prostaglandin synthesis inhibitors in early pregnancy.

When given during the latter part of pregnancy, may cause closure of the fetal ductus arteriosus, oligohydramnios, fetal renal impairment, inhibition of platelet aggregation, and delay labour and birth.

Continuous treatment with NSAIDs during the last trimester of pregnancy should only be given on sound indications. During the last few days before expected birth, agents with an inhibitory effect on prostaglandin synthesis should be avoided.

phenytoin sodium.statement:
Refer to the relevant texts in the Product Information of individual antiepileptics for more information regarding the suitability for use in pregnancy.

Prenatal exposure to phenytoin may increase the risks for congenital malformations and other adverse developmental outcomes. Malformations such as orofacial clefts, cardiac defects, dysmorphic facial features, nail and digit hypoplasia, and growth abnormalities (including microcephaly) have been reported either individually or as part of a fetal Hydantoin Syndrome among children born to women with epilepsy who took phenytoin during pregnancy.
Neurodevelopmental disorder has been reported among children born to women with epilepsy who used phenytoin alone or in combination with other AEDs during pregnancy.
There have been several reported cases of malignancies including neuroblastoma, in children whose mothers received phenytoin during pregnancy. 

Neurodevelopmental disorder has been reported among children born to women with epilepsy who used phenytoin alone or in combination with other antiepileptics during pregnancy. 
Phenytoin can cause coagulation defects with consequent risk of haemorrhage in the fetus and the newborn infant which may be preventable by the prophylactic administration of vitamin K to the mother prior to delivery. 

It is recommended that: 
• Women on antiepileptic drugs (AEDs) receive pre-pregnancy counselling with regard to the risk of congenital malformations or adverse developmental outcomes; 
• AEDs should be continued during pregnancy at the lowest effective dose, and monotherapy should be used if appropriate as risk of congenital malformations or adverse developmental outcomes is greater in women taking combined medication; 
• Adequate folic acid supplementation should be discussed as part of pre-pregnancy and pregnancy counselling; 
• Specialist prenatal diagnosis including detailed mid-trimester ultrasound should be considered.

pimozide.statement: Neonates exposed to antipsychotic drugs during the third trimester of pregnancy are at risk of experiencing extrapyramidal neurological disturbances and/or withdrawal symptoms following delivery. There have been reports of agitation, hypertonia, hypotonia, tremor, somnolence, respiratory distress and feeding disorder in these neonates.

pindolol.statement: Beta-adrenergic blocking agents may cause pharmacological effects such as bradycardia in the fetus and newborn infant.

pipecuronium.statement: There have been no demonstrated adverse effects in the fetus or the newborn infant.

piroxicam.statement:
Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory (NSAIDs) agents inhibit prostaglandin synthesis which may adversely affect pregnancy. Refer to the relevant texts in the Product Information of individual NSAID products for information regarding the suitability for use in pregnancy.
